one_line_takeaway: HATS improves GUI-agent training data by using an MCTS loop that treats instruction-execution misalignment as a 'hardness' reward to steer exploration toward semantically ambiguous actions and iteratively repair synthesized instructions, roughly doubling-to-tripling agent success over OS-Genesis on AndroidWorld and WebArena.
key_finding: Agents SFT-trained on HATS-synthesized data consistently beat all four data-synthesis baselines on both benchmarks and across all three backbones, with the largest gains precisely in the interaction categories where prior pipelines fail. On AndroidWorld overall success rate (InternVL2-4B) HATS reaches 22.60% vs OS-Genesis 11.30% (roughly +100%); on WebArena overall (InternVL2-4B) HATS reaches 20.60% vs OS-Genesis 6.53% (roughly +215%). Ablations show the multi-round refinement is causally important: reconstruction recall rises 0.26 -> 0.40 and success rate 16.00% -> 23.07% from one-shot to round 3, and a recall-based hardness metric (50.8%) outperforms a precision-based one (36.0%). The corpus analysis shows HATS reallocates sampling toward empirically harder categories (P&W 31.2% -> 46.6%) and away from easy ones (S&U 30.8% -> 18.9%).
core_contribution: HATS (Hardness-Aware Trajectory Synthesis) is a framework for automatically synthesizing training trajectories for GUI agents powered by vision-language models. Its own novel contribution is a closed-loop pipeline that explicitly targets 'semantic-ambiguous' actions (interactions whose meaning is context-, sequence-, or visually-dependent), which the authors argue are under-represented in prior synthesis corpora. HATS couples two modules inside a single custom Hardness-Driven Monte Carlo Tree Search (HD-MCTS) loop: (1) a Hardness-Driven Exploration module that replaces OS-Genesis's uniform random UI walk with a UCB-guided search biased toward ambiguous, high-value states; and (2) an Alignment-Guided Refinement module that replaces one-shot reverse instruction generation with a multi-round replay-verify-repair procedure. The key mechanism is an alignment-derived 'hardness reward' r(A,B)=(R(A,B)+eps)^(-alpha) that turns instruction-execution misalignment into a backpropagated search signal, so exploration and supervision-cleanup reinforce each other. NOTE: the OS-Genesis reverse-synthesis paradigm and the two-stage SFT recipe are cited prior work [ref 59]; HATS's distinct additions are the hardness signal, HD-MCTS orchestration, and the multi-round alignment refinement.
threat_model: Not applicable. This is a data-synthesis / agent-training methods paper (cs.CV), not a security paper. It defines no adversary, attack surface, or threat model. 'Robustness' in the paper means generalization of a trained GUI agent to unseen workflows, not adversarial robustness. The dataset's 'Adversarial Robustness' cluster label appears to be a misclassification, likely triggered by the authors' cited prior work on adversarial-attack evaluation (e.g., minimum-margin attack, MMD tests by co-author Ruize Gao) that leaked into the record's abstract field. No security frame should be forced onto this work.
limitations: 1) Heavy dependence on a strong proprietary model (GPT-4o) for instruction synthesis, reward modeling, AND the semantic-ambiguity auditing that validates the central claim, making the evaluation partly self-referential and the pipeline non-self-contained. 2) Absolute success rates remain low (~22-25% overall), so agents are far from reliable despite large relative gains. 3) Component analyses (Tables 3-5, corpus/ambiguity studies) are run on a representative SUBSET of AndroidWorld to save computation, and WebArena is limited to 3 lightweight domains (Gitlab, Maps, Reddit) for cost, weakening breadth of evidence. 4) The category-composition comparison uses Task-Driven-w-Self-Instruct as the baseline (not OS-Genesis, which lacks category-labeled data), a slight apples-to-oranges substitution the authors flag. 5) HATS incurs additional synthesis cost over OS-Genesis (bounded/tunable but not quantified in the main text). 6) No security, safety, or adversarial evaluation is attempted (appropriately, given scope).

datasets_used: AndroidWorld (116 tasks, 4 categories: Daily Life & Services, Social & Communication, System & Utility, Productivity & Work) [ref 48]
WebArena (evaluated on 3 domains: Gitlab, Maps, Reddit) [ref 78]
quantitative_results: AndroidWorld overall SR, InternVL2-4B: HATS 22.60% vs OS-Genesis 11.30% (~+100%)
AndroidWorld overall SR, InternVL2-8B: HATS 24.35% vs OS-Genesis 14.81%
AndroidWorld overall SR, Qwen2-VL-7B: HATS 24.35% vs OS-Genesis 11.50%
AndroidWorld S&C, InternVL2-4B: HATS 38.46% vs OS-Genesis 11.11%; Qwen2-VL-7B: HATS 30.77% vs OS-Genesis 0.00%
WebArena overall SR, InternVL2-4B: HATS 20.60% vs OS-Genesis 6.53% (~+215%)
WebArena overall SR, InternVL2-8B: HATS 24.87% vs OS-Genesis 8.16%
WebArena overall SR, Qwen2-VL-7B: HATS 23.28% vs OS-Genesis 12.81%
WebArena Gitlab, InternVL2-4B: HATS 22.73% vs OS-Genesis 7.94%
Refinement ablation (AndroidWorld): recall R 0.26 -> 0.40 and SR 16.00% -> 23.07% from One-Shot to Round 3
Alignment-metric choice: recall-based hardness 50.8% SR vs precision-based 36.0% SR (Table 4)
Hardness hyperparameter sensitivity (Table 3): default eps=0.01, alpha=1 gives 50.8% SR, best among the grid (others 13.8%-40.0%)
Corpus composition shift: P&W sampling 31.2% -> 46.6%; S&U 30.8% -> 18.9%
Reference (not a competing baseline): GPT-4o M3A zero-shot AndroidWorld overall 45.22%, WebArena overall 22.82%
baselines_compared: GPT-4o M3A agent (reference upper-bound only, not directly comparable)
